Thanks @judyjoseph for the review and the good callout!

You're right that on high-port-count platforms (e.g. 512 SFPs), the SFP Redis reads could add up and potentially delay the next fast-polling cycle. Today each SFP costs 3-5 Redis round-trips per cycle, so at scale that's non-trivial.

That said, this concern exists independent of this PR — the SFP polling already runs in the same thread at 60s today. The per-component interval feature doesn't make it worse; it just makes the impact more visible when you have a fast-polling sensor like ASIC at 5s.

I agree the right approach is to remove SFP temperature polling from thermalctld entirely and let xcvrd own it (there's already a TODO in the code for this). I'd prefer to handle that as a separate PR since it's a different scope of change.

Would it be okay to merge this as-is and I'll file a follow-up to remove the SFP polling path?
Original PR: https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-platform-daemons/pull/635

> @aditya-nexthop @prgeor This change looks correct for fixing the interval anchoring. However, a broader question: if each port takes ~1s to update DOM data and there are 64 ports, the loop takes ~64s per iteration. With DOM_INFO_UPDATE_PERIOD_SECS potentially shorter than that, are we okay with the loop running back-to-back with no sleep/yield? Could there be a concern about starving other tasks or excessive CPU usage in that scenario?

Hi @mihirpat1, as per #758 the wait is primarily due to the time waiting for select to timeout.

We don't need to merge this PR if we merge #758 as #758 includes these changes.
I thought it useful to review the effect of this change as well as #758 independently to understand the impact.

I will close this PR once #758 merges.

> @aditya-nexthop @prgeor This change looks correct for fixing the interval anchoring. However, a broader question: if each port takes ~1s to update DOM data and there are 64 ports, the loop takes ~64s per iteration. With DOM_INFO_UPDATE_PERIOD_SECS potentially shorter than that, are we okay with the loop running back-to-back with no sleep/yield? Could there be a concern about starving other tasks or excessive CPU usage in that scenario?

@mihirpat1 in that case we may have to define `DOM_INFO_UPDATE_PERIOD_SECS ` per platform basis? As the polling period of default 60 seconds does NOT work universally?

> > @aditya-nexthop @prgeor This change looks correct for fixing the interval anchoring. However, a broader question: if each port takes ~1s to update DOM data and there are 64 ports, the loop takes ~64s per iteration. With DOM_INFO_UPDATE_PERIOD_SECS potentially shorter than that, are we okay with the loop running back-to-back with no sleep/yield? Could there be a concern about starving other tasks or excessive CPU usage in that scenario?
> 
> @mihirpat1 in that case we may have to define `DOM_INFO_UPDATE_PERIOD_SECS ` per platform basis? As the polling period of default 60 seconds does NOT work universally?

@prgeor That's correct - defining it per platform can help in addressing this.
@aditya-nexthop can you make this a platform adjusted variable? `DOM_INFO_UPDATE_PERIOD_SECS`

> @aditya-nexthop can you make this a platform adjusted variable? `DOM_INFO_UPDATE_PERIOD_SECS`

Yes, that's a good idea, created https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-platform-daemons/issues/774 to work on this in a new PR.

> @longhuan-cisco can we have an HLD describing this ? We have similar for `GLOBAL_MEDIA_SETTINGS` , `PORT_MEDIA_SETTINGS` [here](https://github.com/sonic-net/SONiC/blob/master/doc/media-settings/Media-based-Port-settings.md). ?
> 

@prgeor 
Sure will update HLD https://github.com/sonic-net/SONiC/blob/master/doc/media-settings/Media-based-Port-settings.md

> We can have `CUSTOM_MEDIA_SETTINGS`

Were you suggesting to put custom attributes in a new `CUSTOM_MEDIA_SETTINGS` section of media_settings.json? I'm not sure about the advantage of doing so.
Instead, by putting all the custom attributes in existing `GLOBAL_MEDIA_SETTINGS` and `PORT_MEDIA_SETTINGS` in the same way as traditional attributes (only difference is the `CUSTOM:` prefix), both traditional and custom attributes are able to share the same parsing logic (without touching the existing main parsing logic), custom attributes automatically obtain all the existing good stuff of today's parser (range ports support, DEFAULT_KEY, vendor+PN matching, regex key matching, media_lane_speed_key, etc) and inherit any potential future enhancement for free.

https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-platform-daemons/blob/99a45940ff92b2513f038c7bf727795b80252e7e/sonic-xcvrd/xcvrd/xcvrd_utilities/media_settings_parser.py#L249-L300

@prgeor 
As discussed offline, putting the combo json-based custom_serdes_attrs string value directly into media_settings.json doesn't seem to be straightforward and would require introducing additional complexity on parser and media_settings.json, to handle breakout scenarios where lanes slicing is needed in today's parser.

Please take a look at the latest PR diff, which has been updated to be more modular based on the latest refactored baseline code. (BTW. Thanks for the really helpful baseline code refactor.)

FYI. In case of you are wondering why I split `get_media_settings_value` into two functions `get_traditional_media_settings_value` and `get_custom_media_settings_value `, please refer to this link for a detailed explanation https://github.com/longhuan-cisco/sonic-platform-daemons/blob/support_custom_serdes_w_docs/docs/why-traditional-and-custom-media-settings-use-separate-lookup-paths-v2.md
